sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A confined space is any enclosed or partially enclosed space that is not designed or intended for continuous human occupancy, has restricted means of entry or exit, and may have a hazardous atmosphere.

**Mine Site Confined Spaces**
Tanks, vessels, silos, storage bins, hoppers, vaults, pits, manholes, tunnels, sewers, pipes, ducting, sumps

**Hazardous Atmospheres**
| Hazard | Threshold |
|---|---|
| Oxygen deficiency | <19.5% O2 |
| Oxygen enrichment | >23.5% O2 (fire/explosion risk) |
| Flammable gas/vapour | >10% of LEL |
| H2S | >10 ppm |
| Carbon monoxide | >25 ppm |
| CO2 | >0.5% |

**Why Confined Spaces Kill**
- Atmosphere may be immediately fatal on entry with no warning
- Oxygen-deficient atmospheres are odourless and colourless
- **Rescuer fatality:** The majority of confined space deaths involve rescuers who entered without protection to assist a collapsed worker
- One victim frequently becomes two, three, or more

sections.title: Recognition
sections.content:
**Signs of an Atmospheric Confined Space Emergency**
- Worker found collapsed, unresponsive, or behaving strangely inside a confined space
- Multiple workers collapsed (atmospheric cause until proven otherwise)
- Worker reports dizziness, headache, or difficulty breathing on entry
- Gas alarm activation within or adjacent to confined space
- Visual signs: smoke, vapour, unusual odours (though odour absence does NOT mean safe)

**Scene Indicators**
- Confined space entry without valid permit
- Atmospheric monitor alarm
- Failed communication with worker inside
- Other workers who entered to assist also collapsed

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
**RESCUER SAFETY - THE MOST IMPORTANT RULE**
**DO NOT ENTER a confined space to rescue a casualty unless you:**
1. Have confirmed atmospheric safety with a calibrated multi-gas monitor, AND
2. Are wearing appropriate respiratory protection (SCBA for unknown/toxic atmospheres), AND
3. Have a standby person outside with SCBA ready, AND
4. Have a means of emergency extraction

**If in doubt - DO NOT ENTER. Call for specialist rescue.**

**Atmospheric Testing**
- Test BEFORE entry; test at multiple heights (H2S and CO2 settle low; flammable gases may stratify)
- Test with monitor held inside before personnel enter
- Continue monitoring throughout any entry
- A valid entry permit requires documented atmospheric results

**Non-Entry Rescue (Always Preferred)**
- If the worker is wearing a harness and lifeline, retrieval may be possible from outside
- Mechanical retrieval equipment (tripod, winch) must be available at confined space entries
- Attempt non-entry rescue first

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
**Immediate Actions**
1. **DO NOT ENTER** - sound alarm, call site emergency number
2. **Activate emergency response** - Mines Rescue, site emergency team, RFDS if required
3. **Attempt non-entry rescue** if worker is wearing a harness/lifeline with retrieval system available
4. **Atmospheric testing** - test before any entry with calibrated multi-gas monitor
5. **Isolate energy sources** - lock out/tag out if possible from outside
6. **Account for all personnel** - confirm how many entered

**If Non-Entry Rescue Achieves Extraction**
- Move patient to fresh air immediately
- Oxygen 15 L/min NRB if unconscious or symptomatic
- Primary survey: ABCDE
- Treat specific toxic gas exposure per relevant guideline (H2S, CO, cyanide)
- Cardiac monitoring
- Establish IV access
- Call RFDS

**Communication**
- Emergency services number: **000** (if on network) or site emergency number
- **RFDS Perth 24/7: 1800 625 800**
- WA Mines Safety: inform of incident

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
**Entry by Trained Rescue Personnel Only**
Entry is only appropriate when:
- Atmospheric testing confirms safe atmosphere, OR
- Appropriately trained personnel with full SCBA are available

**Casualty Management on Extraction**
- Oxygen: 15 L/min NRB; escalate to BVM if apnoeic
- Cardiac arrest: standard CPR; early defibrillation; prolonged resuscitation appropriate in toxicological arrest
- Toxidrome: identify likely atmospheric hazard and treat accordingly
  - O2 deficiency → high-flow O2
  - H2S → high-flow O2 ± hydroxocobalamin
  - CO → 100% O2 (or hyperbaric)
  - Cyanide → hydroxocobalamin
- Spinal precautions if mechanism suggests fall or structural collapse
- IV access, fluid resuscitation if haemodynamically unstable
- Temperature management if prolonged exposure (hypo/hyperthermia)

**Multiple Casualties**
- Triage: START method if >2 casualties
- Call RFDS early - multiple casualty resources required
- Establish clean treatment area away from the confined space

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
**Post-Incident**
- All entrants and rescuers must be medically assessed after any incident
- Confined space must not be re-entered until atmospheric cause is identified and controlled
- Notifiable incident under WA mines safety legislation - report to WA Department of Mines, Industry Regulation and Safety (DMIRS)
- Incident investigation required

**Prevention**
- Written entry permit system for all confined space entries
- Atmospheric testing before and during entry
- Continuous atmospheric monitoring during work
- Standby person and rescue equipment mandatory for all entries
- Emergency rescue plan rehearsed before entry
- SCBA available and personnel trained in its use
